coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent fiber pH sensor for sensitive, wide-range detection using orange-emitting silicon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

Liu, Tengteng

Li, Na
Zhao, Yong
coredata.dc:description: pH value plays a vital role in biochemical analysis. To achieve online analysis, a new type of fluorescent fiber sensor has been developed for real-time, wide-range pH monitoring in biological and environmental fluids. The fluorescent fiber pH sensor was facilely created by immobilizing orange-emitting silicon quantum dots onto a tapered fiber probe through a photoinduced hydrogel polymerization technique. The sensor displays a specific response to pH variations, covering an extensive dynamic range from pH 3.0 to 12.0 with a resolution of 0.06/pH. Additionally, the sensor exhibited remarkable stability, repeatability, robustness, and reversibility. Especially, its high stability to ion strength and temperature fluctuations ensures reliable performance across various physiological and physical conditions. Furthermore, successful implementations of this sensor in detecting pH levels in real serum, artificial sweat and seawater samples further validate its accuracy, practicality and versatility. Overall, this sensor offers a practical and versatile tool for sensitive, real-time pH sensing in complex fluid environments, with great potential across multiple fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical fiber sensor
Wide-range pH sensing
Silicon quantum dots
Online analysis
Fluorescent sensor

F2024501044 and B2022501009 ), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (No. N2423029 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of SiQDs-based fluorescent fiber pH sensor.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of SiQDs. (b) XPS full spectrum of SiQDs. High resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) O 1s and (f) Si 2p.

(a) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of the SiQDs (Inset: photographs of the SiQDs under daylight and UV irradiation at 365 nm). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the SiQDs with a variety of excitation wavelengths. (c) The stability of the SiQDs during the storage. (d) Fluorescent spectra and (e) CIE diagrams of SiQDs before and after solidifying via hydrogel. (f) Photostability of the sensor.

(a) Fluorescent response of the sensor to different pH values. (b) Calibration curve. (c) The bias between the fluorescent fiber sensor and the pH meter for pH detection. (d) Resolution of the fluorescent fiber sensor when detecting pH solutions with intervals of 0.06 pH units. (e) Fluorescence lifetimes of the SiQDs at different pH values.

(a) The response time of the sensor as pH varied from 9.0 to 4.0 and from 4.0 to 9.0 in real time. Effects of (b) NaCl concentration and (c) temperature on the sensor. (d) Reversibility of the sensor under the variation of 4.0 and 9.0 buffers. (e) Stability of the sensor at pH 5.0 for continuous detection in 180s for 90 times. (f) Effects of metal ions and biomolecules on pH sensing. (g) The response variation of three sensors to the pH value of 5.0. The photographs of (h) tapered optical fibers and (i) corresponding fluorescent fiber sensors made from three batches.
Determination of pH in real serum, sweat and seawater samples (n = 3).
Comparison of analytical performance of this sensor with other optical fiber pH sensors.

row.entry: Evanescent wave absorbance fiber optic sensor [10]

head.ce:title: A fluorescent fiber pH sensor for sensitive, wide-range detection using orange-emitting silicon quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Precision Optical Sensing and Measurement Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: pH value plays a vital role in biochemical analysis. To achieve online analysis, a new type of fluorescent fiber sensor has been developed for real-time, wide-range pH monitoring in biological and environmental fluids. The fluorescent fiber pH sensor was facilely created by immobilizing orange-emitting silicon quantum dots onto a tapered fiber probe through a photoinduced hydrogel polymerization technique. The sensor displays a specific response to pH variations, covering an extensive dynamic range from pH 3.0 to 12.0 with a resolution of 0.06/pH. Additionally, the sensor exhibited remarkable stability, repeatability, robustness, and reversibility. Especially, its high stability to ion strength and temperature fluctuations ensures reliable performance across various physiological and physical conditions. Furthermore, successful implementations of this sensor in detecting pH levels in real serum, artificial sweat and seawater samples further validate its accuracy, practicality and versatility. Overall, this sensor offers a practical and versatile tool for sensitive, real-time pH sensing in complex fluid environments, with great potential across multiple fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: pH plays a crucial role in various fields, including biological research [1], clinical science [2], environmental monitoring [3], and food safety [4]. Traditional methods for pH detection include pH test strips and glass electrodes. Commercial pH test strips, which immobilize pH indicators on paper, offer simple operation and low cost, making them popular for visual estimation of pH values, but their measurements are imprecise [5]. Glass pH electrodes, based on the potentiometric method, are considered the gold standard, and widely used due to their accuracy, speed, wide dynamic range, and large-scale production [6]. Nonetheless, these electrodes are prone to corrosion in extremely acidic or alkaline environments. Their relatively large size and rigid structure make them unsuitable for micro-area and small-volume sample analysis, as well as in vivo pH monitoring [7]. Therefore, the development of sensitive, accurate, and miniaturized pH sensors is still in high demand, especially for clinical research and environmental monitoring.

Optical fiber chemical sensors have several main advantages, including compact size, light weight, strong anti-electromagnetic interference, chemical inertness, and superior spatial adaptability and biological compatibility, which render them exceptionally promising for small volume sample analysis, remote sensing, real-time analysis and even in vivo monitoring [7–9]. Optical fiber pH sensors based on absorbance or fluorescence principles are widely used. In these sensors, pH indicators or fluorescence indicators are typically integrated into sensing area of optical fibers, enabling the detection of variations in optical signals with different pH values [10–12]. Fiber optic pH sensors based on absorption are straightforward in design but are generally characterized by low sensitivity. To achieve adequate signal modulation, a high concentration of indicator dyes and a thick sensing layer are required. In contrast, fluorescent fiber sensors leverage the intrinsic benefits of fluorimetry, including high sensitivity and high selectivity, making them advantageous for real-time, in-situ pH monitoring.

The clear distinction between incident light and emission light simplifies signal acquisition and device design. Organic fluorescent pH indicators are commonly used for fluorescent fiber sensing, for example, 1-hydroxypyrene-3,6,8-trisulfonic acid (HPTS, pKa ∼7.3), fluorescein, and carboxyfluorescein (pKa ∼6.5) [12–14]. Lee et al. found that polymer matrix composition could affect the pH detection range of fluorescent fiber pH sensor using 5(6)-carboxynaphthofluorescein. Specifically, sensors utilizing acrylamide exhibited a pH response ranging from 6.6 to 8.0, whereas those based on N-isopropylacrylamide spanned a pH range of 8.1–10.3 [15]. By incorporating an anti-bleaching agent, an HPTS-based optical fiber sensor with a pH range of 5.5–7.4 was developed for real-time, in-vivo monitoring tumor microenvironment pH [16]. However, organic fluorescent dyes-based pH sensors usually respond within a small dynamic scope of maximally 3 pH units. In addition, these sensors encounter problems with poor photostability and small molecule leaching.

Fortunately, the rapid development of high-performance fluorescent nanomaterials, for instance, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) offer good alternatives for biochemical sensing. A few nanomaterials have been explored in fluorescent fiber pH sensors [17–19]. ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs-based fiber pH sensors were fabricated with a resolution of 0.139/pH, accompanying a response range of 6.00–9.01 [18]. A nitrogen‐doped CDs-based fiber optic sensor was developed for online pH sensing, covering the range of 2.00–3.92 and 8.00 to 13.02 [19]. SiQDs, as silicon-based nanomaterials, feature excellent optical properties, high photostability, high biocompatibility, low biotoxicity, and chemical inertness, making them promising candidates for both in vivo and in vitro biosensing [20,21]. Several SiQDs have been successfully applied to pH sensing and imaging [22,23]. However, to the best of our knowledge, the exploration of SiQDs-based optical fiber pH sensors remains seriously insufficient. Biological samples are susceptible to interference from shortwave autofluorescence, so long-wavelength fluorescent nanomaterials are preferred to improve accuracy, increase light penetration depth and reduce phototoxicity. Therefore, the further development of sensitive, robust fluorescent fiber pH sensors covering a wide-region is of considerable significance.

Herein, a fluorescent fiber pH sensor, based on orange-emitting SiQDs, was developed for sensitive pH detection in biological and environmental samples (Fig. 1 ). The SiQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment of neutral red (NR) and N-[3- (trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine (DAMO). The fluorescent fiber pH sensor was fabricated by immobilizing SiQDs on a tapered fiber probe via a UV-induced hydrogel polymerization process. The designed sensor demonstrated a specific response to pH values, a wide dynamic range of pH 3.0 to 12.0, and a high resolution of 0.06/pH. Moreover, the sensor exhibited excellent stability, repeatability, robustness, and reversibility. This sensor was successfully applied to the detection of pH in real serum, artificial sweat, and artificial seawater samples. The sensor provides an accurate and practical approach for real-time, online detection of pH values in various complex samples. It offers a practical and versatile tool for biological research, clinical diagnostics, and environmental monitoring.

DAMO, NR, reduced glutathione (GSH), poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate (PEGDA), l-cysteine (Cys), urea, l-glutamic acid (Glu), 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone (PI), l-serine (Ser), l-lysine (Lys), KCl, MgCl2, ZnCl2, NaCl, FeCl3, CaCl2, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, H3PO4, NaOH, and sodium alginate (SA) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was produced using the HYP-QX-UP ultra-pure water system (Beijing, China). Serum samples were obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Artificial sweat and seawater samples were purchased from Chuangfeng (Dongguan, China).

The instruments used are as follows: LDO-9053A drying oven (Shanghai Longyue, China), PHS-3E pH meter (Shanghai Leici, China), Hitachi 8100 transmission electron microscope and U-5100 absorption spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), Horiba FluoroMax steady-state-transient fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, Japan), Nicolet Avatar 360 infra-red spectrometer and ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA), Shanghai Caikon MCK-6RC optical microscope (Shanghai Caikon, China), 360 nm excitation light source (Shanghai Fiblaser, China), FOFMS filter (Thorlabs, USA), Y-type fiber optic coupler (SUH105/125, Shenzhen Shangpin, China).

The SiQDs were synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal treatment. The detailed synthesis procedure is as follows: Initially, 3 mg of NR, 1 mL of DAMO, and 19 mL of ultra-pure water were thoroughly mixed and moved into an autoclave for hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C with a duration of 6 h. After thorough cooling, the solution was filtered through a hydrophilic filter membrane of 0.22 μm, followed by the addition of ethyl acetate to induce phase separation. Finally, the aqueous phase, containing the SiQDs, was collected and stored at 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of fluorescent fiber pH sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Multimode fiber optic cable (MMF, Wuhan Fiberjump, China) with a core diameter of 105 μm was fabricated to a tapered fiber. Then, the SiQDs-based hydrogel optical fiber probe was prepared by UV-induced in situ polymerization using PEGDA and PI as precursors (Fig. S1) [24]. The detailed information was provided in Supplemental Materials.

In this work, a miniaturized reflective fluorescence optical fiber sensing platform was constructed. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the platform is composed of an excitation light source (λex = 360 nm), a fluorescent optical fiber probe, a Y-type optical fiber coupler, a filter, a spectrometer, and a computer. The filter (cut-off wavelength <400 nm) was adopted to eliminate excitation light and stray light, thereby facilitating background correction.

The SiQDs' morphological feature was examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), exhibiting relatively homogeneous size distribution with an average diameter of approximately 1.5 nm (Fig. S2) and good water dispersibility, which is conducive to their practical applications in the field of biochemical sensing.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) testing were conducted to elucidate the exterior chemical states of the SiQDs. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the broad absorption bands at about 3360 and 3290 cm−1 originated from –OH and –NH2 stretching vibrations. The peak at 2930 cm−1 was produced by C–H bond bending and stretching vibrations. The absorption band at 1589 cm−1 was due to the CN bond stretching vibration, while the bands at 1471 and 1410 cm−1 were related to C–N bonds [25]. The C–O stretching vibration (1309 cm−1) was observed. N–H out-of-plane bending vibration appeared at 781 cm−1. The two sharp peaks, namely at 1123 cm−1 and 1034 cm−1, originated from the Si–O–Si bond stretching vibrations [21]. While that absorption band near 935 cm−1 came from the Si–O–H bond stretching vibration. The FT-IR analysis results indicate that the surface of the fluorescent SiQDs is rich in –OH and –NH2, enhancing their water solubility and stability.

In the full-range XPS spectrum, four peaks were observed at 102.1 eV, 284.9 eV, 398.6 eV, and 531.6 eV (Fig. 2b), individually belonging to the elements Si 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s. The C 1s spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2c, revealed four carbon chemical states: CO (287.8 eV), C–O (286.1 eV), C–N (285.5 eV), and C–C/CC (284.8 eV). In the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d), the three distinct peaks were attributed to NH2, C–N, and pyridinic N bands. In Fig. 2e, three chemical states of oxygen were identified, including C–O at 532.6 eV, Si–O at 531.9 eV, and CO at 530.9 eV. The Si 2p spectrum revealed the characteristic peaks corresponding to Si–O–Si/C at 103 eV, Si–O–H at 102.3 eV (Fig. 2f) [21]. XPS spectral analysis confirms the presence of various functional groups in the synthesized SiQDs, such as –NH2 and-OH, consistent with the FT-IR spectrum analysis.

The optical properties of the SiQDs were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, the SiQDs exhibited two distinct absorption bands, namely, ∼273 nm and ∼450 nm, which were separately originated from the π-π∗ transitions (CC bonds) and n-π∗ transitions (large aromatic structure). The absorption spectra of SiQDs were examined across a variety of pH conditions from acid to base (pH = 4.0, 7.0 and 10.0). The results indicate that the absorption peaks of the SiQDs remain virtually unchanged across these different pH conditions. The SiQDs solution appeared clear orange-red under daylight and emitted intense orange fluorescence under ultraviolet lamp (inset of Fig. 3a). For SiQDs characterization, the fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of the SiQDs solution were tested on a spectrofluorometer. As depicted in Fig. 3b, SiQDs exhibited dual-emission characteristics, with emission peaks located at approximately 430 nm and 590 nm, respectively. When the excitation wavelength increased from 330 nm to 420 nm, the first peak underwent an increase and then a decrease, while the second peak gradually increased. Both peaks exhibited red-shift behavior. When the excitation wavelength surpassed 400 nm, the SiQDs mainly displayed an emission peak at about 590 nm. The results indicate the excitation-dependence of SiQDs.

The robustness of fluorescent nanomaterials is a crucial factor for practical applications. The stability of SiQDs was evaluated during a storage period of 90 days. As depicted in Fig. 3c, SiQDs maintained over 90 % of their fluorescence intensity even after storage for 90 days. The results demonstrate that the synthesized SiQDs have good stability.

The SiQDs-based fluorescent fiber sensor was fabricated as described in Section 2.3 and tested subsequently. Using the fluorescent sensing platform, a slight difference between fluorescence spectra of the SiQDs in free solution and those immobilized in the hydrogel optical fiber was observed. Although spanning the similar emission range, a noticeable blue shift of the emission peak of SiQDs occurred after immobilization (Fig. 3d), namely from 590 nm to 540 nm, which is probably related to the microenvironment change during the immobilization, resulting the emission shift [26]. The Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) diagram confirmed the orange color coordinates of the SiQDs (0.228, 0.527), demonstrating that the SiQDs emitted in the orange region (Fig. 3e). The intense orange fluorescence of the immobilized SiQDs is preferred for sensing in complex samples.

To assess the photostability of the SiQDs-based fiber sensor, the fiber probe was immersed in an aqueous solution and continuously exposed to 360 nm UV light. The results, as shown in Fig. 3f, indicated that even after prolonged exposure to UV light, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor remained stable with almost no significant decay. This demonstrates that the SiQDs-based fluorescent fiber sensor possesses excellent photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of fluorescent fiber pH sensor

ce:section.ce:para: In preliminary experiments, the SiQDs-based fluorescent fiber sensor exhibited a specific response to pH value, rendering it a potential candidate for real-time pH sensing. The response of the sensor to different pH values was investigated. Phosphate buffer (PB, 100 mM) solutions of various pH levels were prepared using NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4, and adjusted with H3PO4 and NaOH solutions. The experiments were carried out at room temperature (25 °C). The efficiency of fluorescence quenching is represented by Rfd, which is calculated as the ratio of fluorescence intensity change (ΔF) to initial intensity (F0). As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescent emission strength of the optical fiber sensor gradually decreased with the increase of pH value. The signal variation depended linearly on pH with a wide dynamic scope of 3.0–12.0 (Fig. 4b). The calibration curve was plotted by Rfd values versus pH values, which was expressed as Y = 0.069X - 0.25 (R2 = 0.991). The bias between the sensor and the pH glass carbon electrode for pH measurement was calculated. The bias values (ΔpH) ranging from −0.38 to 0.09 were assessed at pH values of 4.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0, 10.0, and 11.0 (Fig. 4c). This indicates a good consistency between the sensor and the glass carbon electrode. The sensitivity of the sensor was calculated by referring to the 3σ/s method, and thereinto, s and σ stand for the slope of the linearity curve and standard deviation of durably collecting the sensor's response for 90 times in 180 s, respectively [27]. This sensor presented a high sensitivity of 0.06/pH (σ was obtained at pH = 5.0). To verify the resolution of the sensor, it was tested with several different pH solutions at intervals of 0.06 pH units, namely pH 7.00, 7.06, 7.12, and 7.18. Obvious differences in the sensor's fluorescence intensity and Rfds were observed when it was exposed to the different pH buffer solutions (Fig. 4d). These results indicate that the sensor is capable of distinguishing solutions with a pH interval of 0.06. The results indicate that the sensor has an ultra-wide pH range spanning 10 pH units, significantly broader than that of similar fiber-based pH sensors (Table 2). It will be a universal tool of pH detection in various applications.

The mechanism for pH sensing was subsequently investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3a, the absorption spectra of SiQDs at acidic (pH = 4.0), neutral (pH = 7.0), and basic (pH = 10.0) environments were similar. No obvious changes in absorption bands were observed. The effect of different pH values on the SiQDs' fluorescent lifetime was then examined. In Fig. 4e, the fluorescence decay curves of SiQDs at different pH values were obtained by fitting with a three-exponential function, and the average lifetimes were calculated (the calculation formula is detailed in the Supplemental Materials). The SiQDs' fluorescence lifetimes, as presented in Fig. 4e, reduced gradually as the pH value increased. This outcome can be attributed to the deprotonation of external –NH2 groups of SiQDs in high pH solutions, leading to changes in the Fermi level [28–30]. Additionally, as depicted in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensity increased as the pH value decreased, which may be due to the vibrational coupling effect between surface –OH groups, indirectly promoting the conformational stability and fluorescence emission of SiQDs [31]. Therefore, the mechanism of pH response in SiQDs is due to the protonation-deprotonation of surface –NH2 groups and the vibrational coupling effect of –OH groups.

The sensor's response time was tested by monitoring the fluorescence variation in real time as the pH varied from 9.0 to 4.0 and from 4.0 to 9.0. In Fig. 5 a, the sensor reached complete stability within 34 s, indicating its rapid response to pH variation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature and ion strength
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the temperature ranging from 27 °C to 40 °C on the fluorescent sensor was examined. In Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor remained almost constant with σ ≤ 0.0067, indicating the sensor was stable over a wide temperature range and suitable for pH detection in biological samples. The inertness of the pH sensor to ionic strength was explored via incorporation into various NaCl concentrations ranging from 65 to 520 mM. In Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor changed slightly with increasing NaCl concentration, even at the highest concentration of 520 mM, with variation values all less than 0.011, demonstrating that the sensor exhibits good stability in high salinity environments. The results confirm the sensor is robust and insensitive to ion strength and temperature, beneficial to the implementation under different conditions.

The reversibility of this fluorescent fiber pH sensor was assessed by alternatively immersing in buffered solutions of pH 4.0 and pH 9.0. Over ten cycles, the fluorescence intensity exhibited high reversibility, as depicted in Fig. 5d, with σ ≤ 0.0063, suggesting that the sensor is suitable for repetitive measurements. Meanwhile, the fluorescent sensor exhibited outstanding consistency through the continuous monitoring of its fluorescence fluctuation in a pH 5.0 buffer solution, performed 90 times within 180 s, with σ = 0.0014 (Fig. 5e).

Interference from biological samples, such as inorganic ions and biomolecules, may interfere with the analysis. To assess the specificity of the sensor, the impact of potential interferents in real serum on the fluorescent signal of the fabricated sensor was investigated. The common metal ions (i.e., Fe3+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+) and biomolecules (i.e., urea, Lys, Glu, Cys, Ser, GSH) were tested. As shown in Fig. 5f, the sensor's fluorescence intensity was not significantly affected by the addition of these interferents. This indicates that the sensor exhibits high specificity for pH, making it suitable for pH detection in complex biological samples.

To explore the pH sensor's repeatability, the analytical performances of the fluorescent fiber optic sensors prepared in different batches were tested. The response variations of three sensors to the pH value of 5.0, as depicted in Fig. 5g, were essentially the same, demonstrating that the sensor has good repeatability. At the same time, the morphology and angle of the tapered optical fibers and corresponding hydrogel sensors hardly varied across different batches (Fig. 5h and i), demonstrating that the sensors can be produced in large arrays.

For the validation of this pH sensor's practicality, it was used for detecting pH values in actual serum and artificial sweat samples. The pH values measured by this sensor, as listed in Table 1
, were essentially consistent with those determined by a pH meter, with relative errors less than 1.93 %, demonstrating good practicality and accuracy of this sensor. Moreover, to explore its universality, the sensor was also applied to detect the pH values of seawater samples, indicating its good practicability and universality. These results show that the sensor is suitable for pH determination in complex biological and environmental samples and represents a practical candidate for online, in-situ pH analysis.

The sensor's superiority was assessed by comparing it with other similar fiber pH sensors reported recently (Table 2), demonstrating significant advantages in sensing range, response time and sensitivity, which render the sensor an economic, flexible, and reliable alternative for real-time supervision and emergency analysis.

A novel hydrogel fluorescent fiber sensor based on orange-emitting SiQDs was fabricated for online, sensitive pH detection. The fluorescent SiQDs were facilely synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal method and integrated onto a tapered optical fiber via a photoinduced polymerization reaction to fabricate a hydrogel fluorescent optical fiber sensor. The sensor showed a specific response to pH changes, with an ultra-wide pH range of 3.0–12.0 and a high resolution of 0.06/pH. The sensing mechanism was illustrated in detail. The sensor exhibited excellent selectivity, stability, robustness, reversibility, and repeatability. The sensor was successfully applied to pH detection in complex biological and environmental fluid samples, demonstrating its accuracy, practicality and versatility. Especially, the high robustness of this sensor towards ion strength and temperature highly facilitates its application under different environments. This study provides a practical and versatile method for the real-time, sensitive monitoring of pH values in complex fluid environments, offering potential as a valuable tool for multiple fields.

Dan Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xiaolong Li: Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zijia Huang: Investigation, Data curation. Tengteng Liu: Software, Data curation. Mao Li: Software, Investigation. Xiaoming Chen: Validation. Na Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. Yong Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.62304034), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province of China (Nos. F2024501044 and B2022501009), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (No. N2423029).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2025.344330.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy for the detection of intracellular pH with quantum dot nanosensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Orte, J.M. Alvarez-Pez, M.J. Ruedas-Rama, Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy for the detection of intracellular pH with quantum dot nanosensors, ACS Nano 7 (2013) 6387-6395.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synovial fluid pH sensor for early detection of prosthetic hip infections

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U.N. Wijayaratna, S.D. Kiridena, J.D. Adams, C.J. Behrend, J.N. Anker, Synovial fluid pH sensor for early detection of prosthetic hip infections, Adv. Funct. Mater. 31 (2021) 2104124.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High performance planar pH fluorosensor for two dimensional pH measurements in marine sediment and water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhu, R.C. Aller, Y. Fan, High performance planar pH fluorosensor for two dimensional pH measurements in marine sediment and water, Environ. Sci. Technol. 39 (2005) 8906-8911.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: pH-sensitive (halochromic) smart packaging films based on natural food colorants for the monitoring of food quality and safety

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Alizadeh-Sani, E. Mohammadian, J.-W. Rhim, S.M. Jafari, pH-sensitive (halochromic) smart packaging films based on natural food colorants for the monitoring of food quality and safety, Trends Food Sci. Technol. 105 (2020) 93-144.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of pH test-strip characteristics on accuracy of readings
